elements.sent-2411-001: While 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has recently demonstrated remarkable rendering quality and efficiency in 3D scene reconstruction, it struggles with varying lighting conditions and incidental occlusions in real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-002: To accommodate varying lighting conditions, existing 3DGS extensions apply color mapping to the massive Gaussian primitives with individually optimized appearance embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-003: To handle occlusions, they predict pixel-wise uncertainties via 2D image features for occlusion capture.
elements.sent-2411-004: Nevertheless, such massive color mapping and pixel-wise uncertainty prediction strategies suffer from not only additional computational costs but also coarse-grained lighting and occlusion handling.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this work, we propose a nexus kernel-driven approach, termed {\em NexusSplats , for efficient and finer 3D scene reconstruction under complex lighting and occlusion conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-006: In particular, {\em NexusSplats leverages a novel light decoupling strategy where appearance embeddings are optimized based on nexus kernels instead of massive Gaussian primitives, thus accelerating reconstruction speeds while ensuring local color consistency for finer textures.}
elements.sent-2411-007: Additionally, a Gaussian-wise uncertainty mechanism is developed, aligning 3D structures with 2D image features for fine-grained occlusion handling.
elements.sent-2411-008: Experimental results demonstrate that {\em NexusSplats achieves state-of-the-art rendering quality while reducing reconstruction time by up to 70.4\% compared to the current best in quality.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Reconstructing photorealistic 3D scenes from image collections is a fundamental challenge in computer vision, with applications spanning virtual reality and autonomous driving \cite{chen2022tensorf,yu2024mipsplatting .}
elements.sent-2411-010: The primary objective is to generate multi-view consistent 3D representations from images with known camera poses, enabling photorealistic rendering for unknown viewpoints.
elements.sent-2411-011: As a significant advancement in this field, Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) \cite{mildenhall2021nerf,barron2022mip,tancik2023nerfstudio,rematas2022urban utilizes multilayer perceptrons (MLP) to represent 3D scenes, achieving remarkable rendering quality.}
elements.sent-2411-012: While NeRF operates well in well-controlled image collections, its performance degrades significantly when presented {\em in-the-wild scenarios where lighting conditions vary due to changes in weather, daylight, and camera settings while incidental occlusions appear, such as moving pedestrians and vehicles.}
elements.sent-2411-013: To address these issues, NeRF-W \cite{martin2021nerfw introduces per-image embeddings alongside extra MLPs to perform color mapping for decoupling lighting conditions and predict pixel-wise uncertainties for handling occlusions.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Subsequent works have expanded on NeRF-W in various ways \cite{chen2022hallucinated,rudnev2022relighting,li2023nerfms,yang2023cross,fridovich2023kplanes .}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, these approaches inherit high computational costs from NeRF, limiting their applications.
elements.sent-2411-016: Recently, various works \cite{reiser2021kilonerf,chen2022tensorf,fridovich2022plenoxels,liu2020neural,muller2022instant,sun2022direct,kulhanek2023tetra,kerbl20233d have developed different representations of the 3D scene to enhance the efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Among these methods, 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) \cite{kerbl20233d,yu2024mipsplatting,zwicker2001surface,yu2024gaussianopcacityfields excels in its remarkable efficiency without compromising rendering quality.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Specifically, 3DGS encodes the 3D scene explicitly as a collection of 3D Gaussain primitives and uses rasterization to generate images for given viewpoints.
elements.sent-2411-019: However, similar to NeRF, 3DGS degrades its performance in rendering quality when presented {\em in-the-wild scenarios with complex lighting and occlusion conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Extensions of 3DGS \cite{kulhanek2024wildgaussians,zhang2024gaussianw attempt to address lighting variations by optimizing extra embeddings and performing color mapping on the massive Gaussian primitives, as adaptations of NeRF-based methods.}
elements.sent-2411-021: To handle the occlusions, recent approaches \cite{ren2024nerfonthego,kulhanek2024wildgaussians,zhang2024gaussianw mainly focus on the 2D input images by introducing the latest image segmentation models \cite{wu2020cgnet,caron2021emerging,oquab2023dinov2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Despite the progress, they suffer from two limitations: 1) Extensive computational costs: Typically, representing a large-scale outdoor scene involves millions of Gaussian primitives.
elements.sent-2411-023: Optimizing extra parameters of such an amount significantly slows down the training speed.
elements.sent-2411-024: Moreover, the missing focus on the structural differences between 2D images and the 3D scene limits their effectiveness in handling occlusions.
elements.sent-2411-025: Besides, the clear boundary areas where no occlusions appear are frequently misclassified as occlusions, degrading the reconstruction results.
elements.sent-2411-026: To address these limitations, we propose a nexus kernel-driven approach, called {\em NexusSplats , for efficient and finer 3D scene reconstruction under complex lighting and occlusion conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Specifically, {\em NexusSplats utilizes nexus kernels to divide the 3D scene into voxel segments, enabling hierarchical management for 3D Gaussian primitives.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Each kernel oversees a set of Gaussian primitives within its voxel segment and optimizes an appearance embedding for coordinated color mapping on the associated Gaussian primitives.
elements.sent-2411-029: Instead of optimizing massive appearance embeddings for Gaussian primitives, this coordinated color mapping not only reduces computational costs but also ensures local color consistency when decoupling lighting conditions.
elements.sent-2411-030: For handling occlusions, {\em NexusSplats predicts Gaussian-wise uncertainties and projects them onto pixel-wise uncertainties via rasterization, aligning the structural differences between the 3D scene and 2D images.}
elements.sent-2411-031: To further reduce misidentifications on clear boundary areas, we apply a boundary penalty to the predicted pixel-wise uncertainties, relaxing the focus on clear boundary regions.
elements.sent-2411-032: In summary, our key contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-033: We develop a novel light decoupling strategy that performs coordinated color mapping, efficiently and effectively accommodating varying lighting conditions;.
elements.sent-2411-034: We design the uncertainty splatting mechanism to predict Gaussian-wise uncertainties combined with a boundary penalty, facilitating the handling of occlusions;.
elements.sent-2411-035: Experimental results demonstrate that {\em NexusSplats achieves state-of-the-art rendering quality and reduces reconstruction time in different scenes by up to 70.4\% compared to the current best method in quality.}
elements.sent-2411-036: The primary objective of 3D scene reconstruction is to generate new images from viewpoints that differ from those of the known captures \cite{gortler2023lumigraph,levoy2023light .}
elements.sent-2411-037: NeRF \cite{mildenhall2021nerf,barron2022mip , which employs volume rendering \cite{drebin1988volume,levoy1990efficient,max1995optical , has emerged as a leading methodology in the field.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: However, the computationally intensive MLP optimization used by NeRF limits the reconstruction speed \cite{chen2019learning,mescheder2019occupancy,park2019deepsdf .}
elements.sent-2411-039: To address this, recent works have improved NeRF through enhanced scene representations \cite{chen2022tensorf,fridovich2022plenoxels,kerbl20233d,yu2024mipsplatting,liu2020neural,muller2022instant,sun2022direct .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Notably, 3DGS \cite{kerbl20233d,yu2024mipsplatting achieves remarkable results in rendering quality and efficiency by explicitly representing scenes as collections of 3D Gaussian primitives and leveraging the rasterization process over ray tracing used in NeRF.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Meanwhile, various works \cite{lu2024scaffold,fan2023lightgaussian,lee2024compact,morgenstern2023compact,navaneet2023compact3d,niedermayr2024compressed further extend 3DGS to faster and more compact versions.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Nevertheless, these methods often struggle with {\em in-the-wild scenarios that demand additional handling for varying lighting conditions and incidental occlusions.}
elements.sent-2411-043: In this paper, we investigate robust scene reconstruction techniques tailored for such challenging tasks.
elements.sent-2411-044: NeRF-W \cite{martin2021nerfw first addresses challenges from illumination changes by introducing per-image appearance embeddings and decoupling lighting conditions by mapping colors to adapt to different lighting conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Subsequent works have expanded on NeRF-W's approach in various ways \cite{chen2022hallucinated,rudnev2022relighting,li2023nerfms,yang2023cross,fridovich2023kplanes .}
elements.sent-2411-046: However, these methods inherit NeRF's high computational costs, resulting in slow training and rendering speeds.
elements.sent-2411-047: Recent studies have explored using 3DGS \cite{kerbl20233d,yu2024mipsplatting,zwicker2001surface,yu2024gaussianopcacityfields for {\em in-the-wild scenarios as a faster alternative to NeRF.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Nevertheless, integrating appearance conditioning with locally independent 3D Gaussian primitives presents unique challenges.
elements.sent-2411-049: Existing extensions of 3DGS \cite{zhang2024gaussianw,kulhanek2024wildgaussians typically optimize the appearance embeddings for each Gaussian primitive individually.}
elements.sent-2411-050: However, optimizing these embeddings individually for massive Gaussian primitives incurs huge computational costs, and disrupts local color consistency due to uncoordinated color mapping across Gaussian primitives, leading to both longer training time and distortions in detailed textures.
elements.sent-2411-051: To mitigate the impact of incidental occlusions, such as moving pedestrians and vehicles, pioneering works \cite{martin2021nerfw,chen2022hallucinated leverage trainable embeddings to predict pixel-wise uncertainties as a filtering mask to capture and eliminate occlusions, thereby preventing their models from learning irrelevant objects from training images.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Subsequent methods \cite{yang2023cross,ren2024nerfonthego,kulhanek2024wildgaussians,zhang2024gaussianw improve this process by introducing the latest 2D image models \cite{wu2020cgnet,caron2021emerging,oquab2023dinov2 to compare semantic features between training and predicted images for better occlusion capture.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: However, these methods neglect the structural differences between 2D semantic features and the 3D scene representation.
elements.sent-2411-054: Furthermore, they frequently misidentify clear boundary areas where no occlusions appear due to the inconsistency between training images and predicted images caused by incomplete reconstruction.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1.\linewidth]{images/method/overview.pdf} \caption{Overview of NexusSplats. Left: From the reference image $\mathbf{C}$, we extract light embedding $\mathbf{\varepsilon}_l$ and transient embedding $\mathbf{\varepsilon}_\tau$ to capture global lighting and occlusion conditions. Middle: Our nexus kernels (\cref{sec:nexus kernels}) enable hierarchical management of Gaussian primitives, allowing efficient local adaptations to different lighting and occlusion conditions via the light decoupling module (\cref{sec:light decoupling}) and the uncertainty splatting module (\cref{sec:uncertainty splatting}). Right: Through tile rasterization, we project raw colors $\hat{\mathbf{c}}$, mapped colors $\tilde{\mathbf{c}}$, and uncertainties $\mathbf{\beta}$ onto 2D visible planes. A boundary penalty finally refines the filtering mask in handling occlusions.} \label{fig:overview}
elements.sent-2411-055: In \cref{sec:light decoupling , we detail our light decoupling module, which separates image-dependent lighting conditions, enabling coordinated color mapping.}
elements.sent-2411-056: In this section, we briefly review the process of 3DGS \cite{kerbl20233d , which represents scenes using a set of anisotropic 3D Gaussian primitives, integrating the differential property of volumetric representation with the efficiency of tile-based rasterization.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Starting from Structure-from-Motion (SfM) points \cite{schonberger2016structure , each point is assigned as the position (mean) $\mathbf{\mu}$ of a 3D Gaussian primitive: \begin{equation}\mathcal{G}(\mathbf{x})=e^{-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{\mu})^\top\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{\mu})} , \label{eq:gaussian}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{x}$ is the position in the 3D scene, and $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ denotes the covariance matrix of the 3D Gaussian primitive.}
elements.sent-2411-058: To ensure $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ remains positive semi-definite, it is constructed using a scaling matrix $\mathbf{S}$ and rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}$: \begin{equation}\mathbf{\Sigma}=\mathbf{R}\mathbf{S}\mathbf{S}^\top\mathbf{R}^\top . \label{eq:covariance}\end{equation} Each 3D Gaussian primitive is associated with a color $\hat{\mathbf{c }$, represented by spherical harmonics (SH), and an opacity $\alpha$ that scales $\mathcal{G (\mathbf{x )$ during blending.}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Unlike traditional volumetric methods, 3DGS uses tile-based rasterization instead of computationally expensive ray tracing.
elements.sent-2411-060: Each 3D Gaussian primitive $\mathcal{G (\mathbf{x )$ is projected into a 2D Gaussian primitive $\mathcal{G '(\mathbf{x )$ on the image plane, following the projection process outlined in this work \cite{zwicker2001ewa .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: The tile-based rasterizer then sorts the 2D Gaussian primitives efficiently and applies $\alpha$-blending: \begin{equation}\hat{\mathbf{C}}(\mathbf{x}')=\sum_{i\in N}\hat{\mathbf{c}}_i\sigma_i\prod_{j=1}^{i-1}(1-\sigma_j),\quad\sigma_i=\alpha_i\mathcal{G}_i'(\mathbf{x}') , \label{eq:splatting}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{x '$ is the pixel position, and $N$ denotes the number of sorted 2D Gaussian primitives corresponding to that pixel.}
elements.sent-2411-062: With the differentiable property, all attributes of 3D Gaussian primitives are learnable and optimized end-to-end via training view reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-063: The final rendered colors $\hat{\mathbf{C }$ are computed using a weighted combination of DSSIM \cite{wang2004ssim and $L_1$ losses with the reference colors $\mathbf{C}$: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}=\lambda \cdot \mathrm{DSSIM}(\hat{\mathbf{C}},\mathbf{C})+(1-\lambda)\|\hat{\mathbf{C}}-\mathbf{C}\|_1 , \label{eq:3dgs-loss}\end{equation} where $\lambda$ is set to 0.2 as a hyper-parameter.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: This section introduces our nexus kernels, which enable hierarchical management for Gaussian primitives in the reconstructed scene.
elements.sent-2411-065: We first divide the 3D scene into manageable voxel segments based on nexus kernels.
elements.sent-2411-066: Each kernel oversees a set of Gaussian primitives within its voxel segment, predicting the Gaussian-specific attributes for rasterization while enabling coordinated color mapping and Gaussian-wise uncertainty prediction.
elements.sent-2411-067: Specifically, each kernel is equipped with a local appearance embedding $\mathbf{\varepsilon _a$ and an uncertainty embedding $\mathbf{\varepsilon _\beta$.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: The appearance embedding $\mathbf{\varepsilon _a$ captures local lighting response patterns to global illumination changes, while the uncertainty embedding $\mathbf{\varepsilon _\beta$ estimates the likelihood of occlusion occurrence in the voxel segment.}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Following standard practices in scene representation \cite{kerbl20233d,xu2022point , we initialize nexus kernels using a sparse point cloud generated by COLMAP \cite{schonberger2016structure , with additional kernels positioned on a surrounding sphere to model the sky.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: Each kernel $u$ is assigned a voxel segment of the 3D scene, overseeing $h$ Gaussian primitives within the voxel segment.
elements.sent-2411-071: By optimizing a local context feature vector $\mathbf{f _u$, a scaling factor $k_u$, and learnable offsets $\{\mathcal{O _i\}_{i=1 ^h$, each kernel controls the positions and other attributes of $h$ Gaussian primitives.}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: The positions $\{\mathbf{\mu _i\}_{i=1 ^h$ are calculated by: \begin{equation}\mathbf{\mu}_i=\mathbf{x}_u+\mathcal{O}_i\cdot k_u , \label{eq:offsets_position}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{x _u$ is the central position of kernel $u$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: Other attributes, i.e. opacity $\alpha$, covariance matrix $\mathbf{\Sigma}$, scaling $\mathbf{s}$, and color $\hat{\mathbf{c }$, are predicted by Kernel MLPs denoted as $F_\alpha$, $F_\sigma$, $F_s$ and $F_c$.}
elements.sent-2411-074: To provide the light decoupling module with a more suitable color expression, we modify the color expression in 3DGS by fixing the color channels to three and setting the SH degree to one.
elements.sent-2411-075: However, simply fixing the color channels can lead to view inconsistency because of reduced dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-076: Therefore, we additionally pass the viewing direction $\vec{d _{uv}$ to $F_c$ to ensure the view consistency.}
elements.sent-2411-077: For a camera located at position $\mathbf{x _v$, each Gaussian primitive's color $\hat{\mathbf{c }\in\mathbb{R ^3$ can be predicted as: \begin{equation}\{\hat{\mathbf{c}}_i\}_{i=1}^h=F_c(\mathbf{f}_u,\vec{d}_{uv}) , \quad\vec{d}_{uv}=\frac{\mathbf{x}_u-\mathbf{x}_v}{\|\mathbf{x}_u-\mathbf{x}_v\|_2} , \label{eq:color_prediction}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{x _u$ denotes the location of nexus kernel $u$, and $\mathbf{f _u$ denotes the per-kernel context feature vector.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: The remaining attributes of Gaussian primitives are predicted similarly as \cref{eq:color_prediction .}
elements.sent-2411-079: To relieve the computational cost of optimizing extensive attributes and embeddings, we apply a pruning process to eliminate redundant kernels.
elements.sent-2411-080: By accumulating the opacity values of the Gaussian primitives associated with a nexus kernel, we eliminate it and its associated Gaussian primitives if the accumulated opacity is below a threshold.
elements.sent-2411-081: Additionally, to encourage compact scene representations with minimal overlap of Gaussian primitives, a regulation loss is introduced: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_\mathrm{reg}=\sum_{i=1}^{N\times h}\mathrm{Prod}(\mathbf{s}_i) , \label{eq:reg_loss}\end{equation} where $N$ represents the total number of nexus kernels, $\mathrm{Prod (\cdot)$ denotes the product of a vector's values, and $\mathbf{s}$ is the scaling factor of each Gaussian primitive.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Leveraging the hierarchical management for Gaussian primitives, we perform coordinated color mapping to decouple lighting conditions from training images.
elements.sent-2411-083: Our light decoupling module incorporates three components: an image-specific light embedding $\mathbf{\varepsilon _l$, a kernel-specific appearance embedding $\mathbf{\varepsilon _a$, and a decoupling MLP $F_\theta$.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: The light embedding $\mathbf{\varepsilon _l$ captures the global lighting conditions of training images, while the appearance embedding $\mathbf{\varepsilon _a$ learns the localized response to global lighting variations.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Using these embeddings, we transform the raw colors $\{\hat{\mathbf{c _i\}_{i=1 ^h$ of Gaussian primitives into mapped colors $\{\tilde{\mathbf{c _i\}_{i=1 ^h$ via $F_\theta$: \begin{equation}\{\tilde{\mathbf{c}}_i\}_{i=1}^h= F_\theta(\{\hat{\mathbf{c}}_i\}_{i=1}^h, \mathbf{\varepsilon}_a^{(u)}, \mathbf{\varepsilon}_l^{(v)}, \vec{d}_{uv}) , \label{eq:color-mapping}\end{equation} where $h$ denotes the number of offsets associated with nexus kernel $u$, and $v$ marks the specific training image.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: To maintain view consistency after the coordinated color mapping, we additionally input the viewing direction $\vec{d _{uv}$ into $F_\theta$, similar to \cref{eq:color_prediction .}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Furthermore, we set a dropout rate of 0.2 within $F_\theta$ to mitigate overfitting in decoupling lighting conditions.
elements.sent-2411-088: Both raw colors $\hat{\mathbf{c }$ and mapped colors $\tilde{\mathbf{c }$ are projected onto 2D planes as $\hat{\mathbf{C }$ and $\tilde{\mathbf{C }$, respectively, using \cref{eq:splatting .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Consistent with 3DGS, we train the model using a combination of DSSIM \cite{wang2004ssim and $L_1$ losses (\cref{eq:3dgs-loss ).}}
elements.sent-2411-090: Given the reference colors $\mathbf{C}$ from the training images, we compute the $L_1$ loss between $\mathbf{C}$ and mapped colors $\tilde{\mathbf{C }$, guiding the light decoupling module to capture image-specific lighting effects.}
elements.sent-2411-091: Additionally, we compute the DSSIM loss directly on raw colors $\hat{\mathbf{C }$ to leverage its robustness to changes in appearance colors, focusing primarily on structural and perceptual similarity.}
elements.sent-2411-092: The training loss for colors is formulated as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_\mathrm{color}=\lambda\cdot\mathrm{DSSIM}(\hat{\mathbf{C}},\mathbf{C})+(1-\lambda)\|\tilde{\mathbf{C}}-\mathbf{C}\|_1 . \label{eq:decoupling-loss}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-093: This section elaborates on our design for handling occlusions, termed uncertainty splatting.
elements.sent-2411-094: We align the pixel-wise uncertainties with the 3D scene representation, i.e. the distributed 3D Gaussian primitives.
elements.sent-2411-095: In particular, we propagate the pixel-wise uncertainties to each Gaussian primitive and splat them onto visible planes as the filtering mask through rasterization.
elements.sent-2411-096: In parallel with the light decoupling module, we predict Gaussian-wise uncertainties $\{\mathbf{\beta _i\}_{i=1 ^h$ using three components: an image-level transient embedding $\mathbf{\varepsilon _\tau$, a kernel-level uncertainty embedding $\mathbf{\varepsilon _\beta$, and an uncertainty MLP $F_\beta$: \begin{equation}\{\mathbf{\beta}_i\}_{i=1}^h= F_\beta(\mathbf{\varepsilon}_\beta^{(u)}, \mathbf{\varepsilon}_\tau^{(v)}) . \label{eq:uncertainty-predicting}\end{equation} Through rasterization (\cref{eq:splatting ), we project per-Gaussian uncertainties $\mathbf{\beta}$ onto 2D planes to produce pixel-wise uncertainties $\hat{\mathbf{\beta }$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: By modeling observed colors $\mathbf{C}$ with an isotropic normal distribution with mean $\tilde{\mathbf{C }$ and variance $\hat{\mathbf{\beta }$, we optimize predicted uncertainties $\hat{\mathbf{\beta }$ by minimizing the negative log-likelihood of the normal distribution: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_u = \frac{\|\tilde{\mathbf{C}}-\mathbf{C}\|_2^2}{2\hat{\mathbf{\beta}}^2}+\lambda_1\mathrm{log}\hat{\mathbf{\beta}} , \label{eq:log-likelihood}\end{equation} where $\lambda_1$ is a regression hyper-parameter, set to 0.5 in our experiments.}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: However, the standard $L_2$ loss between $\tilde{\mathbf{C }$ and $\mathbf{C}$ has limited effectiveness in identifying occlusions that are not part of the static scene \cite{ren2024nerfonthego,kulhanek2024wildgaussians .}}
elements.sent-2411-099: To address this, we replace the $L_2$ loss with a cosine similarity $\mathcal{D}$ between the semantic feature vectors of rendered image $\tilde{\mathbf{C }$ and reference image $\mathbf{C}$: \begin{equation}\mathcal{D}{(\tilde{\mathbf{C}},\mathbf{C})}=\sum_{i=1}^P\frac{\tilde{\mathcal{E}}_i\cdot \mathcal{E}_i}{\|\tilde{\mathcal{E}}_i\|_2 \|\mathcal{E}_i\|_2}, \label{eq:dino-similarity}\end{equation} where $\tilde{\mathcal{E }$ and $\mathcal{E}$ are the patch-wise feature vectors extracted by a pre-trained DINO model \cite{caron2021emerging,oquab2023dinov2 , and $P$ is the total number of patches.}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: This adjusted loss function allows our method to better capture occlusions from training images.
elements.sent-2411-101: Nevertheless, our method also tends to misidentify clear boundary regions as occlusions.
elements.sent-2411-102: These misidentifications can degrade reconstruction quality, bringing drawbacks to occlusion handling.
elements.sent-2411-103: Due to the insufficient representation of Gaussian primitives alongside the lack of observations in training images, the boundary areas of a reconstructed 3D scene often present lower rendering quality, leading to different semantic features between training and predicted images.
elements.sent-2411-104: Therefore, we apply a boundary penalty $\mathcal{M}$ to the predicted pixel-wise uncertainties $\hat{\beta}$ to loosen the criteria at the boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-105: For a pixel at $(x, y)$, $\mathcal{M}$ is modeled as a 2D Gaussian distribution: \begin{equation}\mathcal{M}(x,y) = \exp\left(-\frac{(x-\bar{x})^2}{2\sigma_x^2}-\frac{(y-\bar{y})^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right), \label{eq:boundary-penalty}\end{equation} where $\bar{x}$ and $\bar{y}$ represent the distribution center, and $\sigma_x^2$ and $\sigma_y^2$ are variances along the $x$ and $y$ axes.
elements.sent-2411-106: Our final training objective combines all the loss terms as follows: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}=\frac{\mathcal{L}_\mathrm{color}}{2\hat{\mathbf{\beta}}^2\mathcal{M}} +\mathcal{L}_u + \lambda_\mathrm{reg}\mathcal{L}_\mathrm{reg}, \label{eq:final-loss}\end{equation} where $\lambda_\mathrm{reg}$ is set to 0.01 in our experiments, and the pixel-wise uncertainties $\hat{\mathbf{\beta }$ restricted by the boundary penalty $\mathcal{M}$ act as filters on the color loss $\mathcal{L _\mathrm{color}$, excluding imperfect pixels that contain occlusions during optimization.}}

elements.sent-2411-178: Photorealistic 3D reconstruction of unstructured real-world scenes remains challenging due to complex illumination variations and transient occlusions.
elements.sent-2411-179: Existing methods based on Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) and 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) struggle with inefficient light decoupling and structure-agnostic occlusion handling.
elements.sent-2411-180: To address these limitations, we propose NexusSplats, an approach tailored for efficient and high-fidelity 3D scene reconstruction under complex lighting and occlusion conditions.
elements.sent-2411-181: In particular, NexusSplats leverages a hierarchical light decoupling strategy that performs centralized appearance learning, efficiently and effectively decoupling varying lighting conditions.
elements.sent-2411-182: Furthermore, a structure-aware occlusion handling mechanism is developed, establishing a nexus between 3D and 2D structures for fine-grained occlusion handling.
elements.sent-2411-183: Experimental results demonstrate that NexusSplats achieves state-of-the-art rendering quality and reduces the number of total parameters by 65.4\%, leading to 2.7$\times$ faster reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-184: Photorealistic 3D scene reconstruction from unstructured image collections remains a cornerstone for applications ranging from virtual reality to autonomous navigation \cite{martin2021nerfw,chen2022hallucinated .}
elements.sent-2411-185: While Neural Radiance Fields (NeRFs) \cite{mildenhall2021nerf,barron2022mip,tancik2023nerfstudio,rematas2022urban achieve impressive view synthesis in controlled settings, their volumetric rendering struggles with in-the-wild scenarios characterized by illumination variations (e.g. weather changes) and transient occlusions (e.g. moving pedestrians).}
elements.sent-2411-186: NeRF-W \cite{martin2021nerfw pioneers light decoupling and occlusion handling through image-specific appearance and transient embeddings combined with color mapping and 2D uncertainty estimation.}
elements.sent-2411-187: Follow-up works \cite{chen2022hallucinated,rudnev2022relighting,li2023nerfms,yang2023cross,kassab2023refinedfields enhance the appearance and uncertainty modeling by introducing various image feature extractors, such as CNN and U-Net.}
elements.sent-2411-188: However, these methods inherit NeRF's prohibitive computational cost, requiring hours of training per scene.
elements.sent-2411-189: The emergence of 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) \cite{kerbl20233d,yu2024mipsplatting,yu2024gaussianopcacityfields revolutionizes the field with explicit 3D Gaussians and differentiable rasterization \cite{zwicker2002ewa , enabling faster reconstruction with real-time rendering.}}
elements.sent-2411-190: Recent adaptations \cite{kulhanek2024wildgaussians,zhang2024gaussianw,xu2024splatfacto,xu2024wildgs extend 3DGS to in-the-wild scenes by introducing per-Gaussian appearance embeddings and scattered color mapping to decouple lighting conditions from each explicit Gaussian primitive.}
elements.sent-2411-191: To handle transient occlusions, these methods remain their focus on advanced image feature extractors for uncertainty modeling.
elements.sent-2411-192: Despite progress in improving rendering quality, two fundamental limitations persist: (1) Inefficient light decoupling.
elements.sent-2411-193: Representing outdoor scenes with millions of 3D Gaussians, current methods attach independent appearance embeddings to the substantial number of Gaussians.
elements.sent-2411-194: This scattered appearance learning increases the total number of parameters and slows training, while simplified alternatives like positional encodings \cite{dahmani2024swag sacrifice texture fidelity due to insufficient learning of detailed appearance features.}
elements.sent-2411-195: Existing 2D-centric occlusion detection fails to account for the inherent structural discrepancies between 2D input images and the 3D representation, limiting the accuracy in capturing occlusions.
elements.sent-2411-196: Besides, clear scene boundaries where no occlusions appear are frequently misclassified as occlusions by existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-197: Excluding clear areas while retaining uncaptured occlusions leads to incomplete scene reconstruction with artifacts and shadows.
elements.sent-2411-198: The key to addressing these issues lies in two key perspectives: (1) Construct an efficient structure that learns sufficient appearance details while scaling down the parameters.
elements.sent-2411-199: Based on this, we propose NexusSplats that performs hierarchical light decoupling and structure-aware occlusion handling.
elements.sent-2411-200: To enable hierarchical light decoupling, we introduce the hierarchical and region-aware 3D representation \cite{lu2024scaffold to organize the 3D Gaussians into nexus kernels --- dynamic 3D primitives tailored for in-the-wild scene reconstruction.}
elements.sent-2411-201: In particular, each nexus kernel learns a shared appearance embedding and coordinates color mapping for its managed Gaussians, allowing for centralized appearance learning while scaling down the number of appearance embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-202: This design maintains sufficient detail learning and ensures rendering quality.
elements.sent-2411-203: Regarding occlusion handling, a shared uncertainty embedding is also assigned for each kernel to predict the 3D uncertainties of the associated Gaussians.
elements.sent-2411-204: These 3D uncertainties are then projected onto image planes via tile rasterization, propagating to 2D uncertainty masks.
elements.sent-2411-205: To further correct misidentifications on clear scene boundaries, we apply a boundary-aware refinement to the 2D uncertainty masks.
elements.sent-2411-206: Combined with the 2D semantic feature loss \cite{ren2024nerfonthego,kulhanek2024wildgaussians , our uncertainty propagation mechanism bridges 3D uncertainties with 2D semantic features, improving the accuracy of capturing true occlusions.}
elements.sent-2411-207: Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method not only significantly reduces redundant parameters but also presents finer textures and a more accurate removal of occlusions.
elements.sent-2411-208: We develop a hierarchical light decoupling strategy that performs centralized appearance learning, efficiently and effectively decoupling varying lighting conditions.
elements.sent-2411-209: We design a structure-aware occlusion handling mechanism that establishes a nexus between 3D and 2D structures, improving the accuracy of occlusion captures.
elements.sent-2411-210: Experimental results demonstrate that NexusSplats achieves state-of-the-art rendering quality and reduces total parameters by 65.4\%, which leads to 2.7$\times$ faster reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-211: NeRFs \cite{mildenhall2021nerf,barron2022mip,tancik2023nerfstudio,rematas2022urban revolutionize 3D scene reconstruction through continuous volumetric representations, enabling photorealistic novel view synthesis.}
elements.sent-2411-212: However, their reliance on ray tracing and MLP-based rendering imposes prohibitive computational costs \cite{chen2019learning,mescheder2019occupancy,park2019deepsdf .}
elements.sent-2411-213: Subsequent works accelerate NeRFs through enhanced scene representations \cite{chen2022tensorf,fridovich2022plenoxels,kerbl20233d,liu2020neural,muller2022instant,sun2022direct , where 3DGS \cite{kerbl20233d,yu2024mipsplatting,yu2024gaussianopcacityfields excels in its remarkable efficiency without compromising rendering quality by combining explicit 3D Gaussians with differentiable rasterization.}}
elements.sent-2411-214: While follow-up works \cite{lu2024scaffold,lin2024vastgaussian,fan2023lightgaussian,lee2024compact,morgenstern2023compact,navaneet2023compact3d,niedermayr2024compressed further compress 3DGS representations for settling large-scale scenes, they remain inadequate in handling real-world challenges like complex illumination variations and transient occlusions.}
elements.sent-2411-215: In this paper, we investigate scene representations tailored for such challenging conditions.
elements.sent-2411-216: To handle illumination variations, NeRF-based methods \cite{martin2021nerfw,chen2022hallucinated,rudnev2022relighting,li2023nerfms,yang2023cross,kassab2023refinedfields typically employ image-specific embeddings and neural feature extractors to disentangle appearance changes.}
elements.sent-2411-217: However, these approaches inherit NeRF's computational inefficiency, resulting in prohibitive training and rendering times.
elements.sent-2411-218: Recent efforts \cite{zhang2024gaussianw,kulhanek2024wildgaussians,xu2024splatfacto,xu2024wildgs integrate appearance conditioning with 3DGS by introducing per-Gaussian appearance embeddings and scattered color mapping.}
elements.sent-2411-219: Though proven to be effective, optimizing such a substantial number of extra parameters significantly increases computational costs.
elements.sent-2411-220: While SWAG \cite{dahmani2024swag proposes positional encodings to replace per-Gaussian embeddings for higher efficiency, this strategy sacrifices texture fidelity due to limited appearance modeling capacity.}
elements.sent-2411-221: Our approach strikes a novel balance between efficiency and quality by introducing a hierarchical light decoupling strategy that maintains high-fidelity rendering without excessive parameters.
elements.sent-2411-222: To mitigate the impact of transient occlusions, such as moving pedestrians and vehicles, pioneering works \cite{martin2021nerfw,chen2022hallucinated,yang2023cross,sabour2023robustnerf either predict 2D uncertainty masks or leverage loss controls based on image feature extractors (e.g. CNN and U-Net) to capture and eliminate occlusions, thereby preventing their models from learning irrelevant objects from training images.}
elements.sent-2411-223: Subsequent methods \cite{ren2024nerfonthego,kulhanek2024wildgaussians enhance occlusion detection using advanced semantic feature extractors like DINO \cite{caron2021emerging,oquab2023dinov2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-224: However, their reliance on 2D-informed guidance can lead to inconsistencies with the underlying 3D scene geometry, making them more prone to occasional false or missed occlusion identifications.
elements.sent-2411-225: In addition, specialized 3DGS variants for occlusion handling \cite{sabourgoli2024spotlesssplats,lin2024hybridgs primarily focus on small-scale indoor scenarios, which involve occlusion challenges but lack drastic illumination variations in real-world outdoor environments.}
elements.sent-2411-226: In contrast, our method introduces a structure-aware occlusion handling framework designed for large outdoor scenes, where complex illumination changes and transient objects coexist.
elements.sent-2411-227: This dual consideration of lighting and occlusion distinguishes our approach from existing solutions that address transient occlusions in isolation.
elements.sent-2411-228: Our approach comprises two key components: (1) Hierarchical Light Decoupling (\Cref{sec:light decoupling ) that leverages nexus kernels to hierarchically manage 3D Gaussians and perform centralized appearance learning for efficient and effective light condition decoupling.}
elements.sent-2411-229: Using a combination of color loss and uncertainty loss, NexusSplats enables delicate scene reconstructions with steerable lighting conditions via unified optimization (\Cref{sec:optimization ).}
elements.sent-2411-230: In this section, we detail each component of NexusSplats, elaborating on how they collectively contribute to efficient and high-quality 3D scene reconstruction under complex conditions.
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=1.\linewidth]{images/method/overview.pdf} \caption{Overview of NexusSplats. Our framework operates in three stages: First, the hierarchical Gaussian management (\Cref{sec:hierarchical management}) organizes 3D Gaussians into dynamic nexus kernels, which generate Gaussian attributes and perform centralized appearance learning (\Cref{sec:centralized appearance learning}) and uncertainty propagation (\Cref{sec:uncertainty propagation}). Second, a raw image $\hat{\mathbf{C}}$, a mapped image $\tilde{\mathbf{C}}$, and an uncertainty mask $\frac{1}{2\hat{\mathbf{\beta}}^2}$ are rendered through tile rasterization. Third, the boundary-aware refinement (\Cref{sec:boundary-aware refinement}) corrects misclassified scene boundaries. The system optimizes via a combination of color loss $\mathcal{L}_c$ and uncertainty loss $\mathcal{L}_u$.} \label{fig:overview}
elements.sent-2411-231: In this section, we briefly review 3DGS \cite{kerbl20233d and its extension Scaffold-GS \cite{lu2024scaffold , which jointly serve as a basis of our approach.}}
elements.sent-2411-232: These methods combine explicit 3D Gaussian representations with differentiable rasterization for efficient scene reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-233: Each Gaussian $\mathcal{G (\mathbf{x )$ is defined by: \begin{equation}\mathcal{G}(\mathbf{x})=e^{-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{\mu})^\top\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{\mu})} ,\quad\mathbf{\Sigma}=\mathbf{R}\mathbf{S}\mathbf{S}^\top\mathbf{R}^\top, \label{eq:gaussian}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{\mu}$ is the centroid position of a 3D Gaussian, and $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ encodes anisotropic covariance via a scaling matrix $\mathbf{S}$ and a rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-234: Each Gaussian also stores the view-dependent color $\hat{\mathbf{c }$, modeled by spherical harmonics, and an opacity $\alpha$.}
elements.sent-2411-235: Unlike traditional volumetric methods, 3DGS uses tile-based rasterization \cite{zwicker2002ewa for real-time rendering.}
elements.sent-2411-236: Each 3D Gaussian $\mathcal{G (\mathbf{x )$ is projected onto image planes, resulting in a 2D Gaussian $\mathcal{G '(\mathbf{x ')$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-237: The 2D Gaussians are then sorted and splatted via $\alpha$-blending: \begin{equation}\hat{\mathbf{C}}(\mathbf{x}')=\sum_{i\in N}\hat{\mathbf{c}}_i\sigma_i\prod_{j=1}^{i-1}(1-\sigma_j),\quad\sigma_i=\alpha_i\mathcal{G}_i'(\mathbf{x}') , \label{eq:splatting}\end{equation} where $\sigma_i$ denotes the contribution weight of the $i$-th Gaussian to pixel $\mathbf{x '$.}
elements.sent-2411-238: With the differentiable property, all attributes of 3D Gaussians are learnable and optimized end-to-end during training.
elements.sent-2411-239: Instead of directly optimizing a large, unstructured set of Gaussians, Scaffold-GS organizes the scene using a sparse grid of anchor points $\{\mathcal{V _i\}$, initialized from SfM point clouds and dynamically generating $h$ neural Gaussians per anchor: \begin{equation}\{\mathbf{\mu}_j\}_{j=1}^h = \mathbf{x}_i + \{\mathcal{O}_j\}_{j=1}^h \cdot l_v, \label{eq:neural_gaussian_position}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{x _i$ is the position of anchor $\mathcal{V _i$, and $\{\mathcal{O _j\}$ and $l_v$ are learnable offsets and scaling factors.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-240: To generate the attributes of each neural Gaussian, Scaffold-GS employs respective MLPs for prediction.
elements.sent-2411-241: Notably, the spherical harmonics with dynamic color channels are replaced with neural networks with fixed channels, consistent with other Gaussian attributes.
elements.sent-2411-242: Specifically, given viewing position $\mathbf{x _v$, anchor $\mathcal{V _i$ predicts the colors $\{\hat{\mathbf{c _j\}_{j=1 ^{h}$ of neural Gaussians by: \begin{equation}\{\hat{\mathbf{c}}_j\}_{j=1}^{h}=F_c(\mathbf{f}_{v},\vec{d}_{iv}),\quad\vec{d}_{iv}=\frac{\mathbf{x}_i-\mathbf{x}_v}{\|\mathbf{x}_i-\mathbf{x}_v\|_2}, \label{eq:neural_gaussian_color}\end{equation} where $F_{c}$ is the color MLP shared by all anchors and the viewing direction $\vec{d _{iv}$ ensures viewing consistency.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-243: Analogous MLPs, $F_\alpha$, $F_q$, and $F_s$, are used for opacity, covariance matrix, and scaling prediction.
elements.sent-2411-244: Finally, a unique optimization process is developed to prune redundant anchors with associated neural Gaussians and reduce total parameters.
elements.sent-2411-245: This section introduces our hierarchical light decoupling strategy that consists of hierarchical Gaussian management and centralized appearance learning.
elements.sent-2411-246: This design effectively reduces parameter redundancy while achieving superior texture fidelity under varying illumination.
elements.sent-2411-247: The core objective of hierarchical light decoupling is to establish an efficient representation that learns sufficient appearance features across 3D Gaussians while minimizing parameter overhead.
elements.sent-2411-248: A natural strategy involves reducing the number of appearance embeddings by grouping spatially adjacent Gaussians with similar appearance characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-249: However, roughly clustering Gaussian primitives based on their positions and colors suffers from two critical limitations.
elements.sent-2411-250: First, leveraging a static clustering approach, where 3D Gaussians are roughly grouped based on their initial positions and colors, fails to capture the dynamic changes in Gaussian attributes (e.g. position, opacity, color) during training, leading to inconsistent appearance groupings.
elements.sent-2411-251: Second, introducing dynamic clustering requires frequent re-clustering which incurs prohibitive computational costs due to pairwise similarity calculations across millions of Gaussians.
elements.sent-2411-252: To address these challenges, we draw inspiration from the hierarchical organization strategy of Scaffold-GS \cite{lu2024scaffold and utilize it for Gaussian management.}
elements.sent-2411-253: Expanding on the concept of anchor points, we introduce nexus kernels, which inherit the neural Gaussian generation capability and learn detailed appearance features in a centralized manner.
elements.sent-2411-254: In this way, each nexus kernel ensures the appearance similarities of the neural Gaussians it spawns, paving the way for centralized appearance learning.
elements.sent-2411-255: Equipped with the shared appearance embedding $\mathbf{\varepsilon _a^{(i)}$, each nexus kernel $\mathcal{U _i$ learns centralized appearance features and coordinates color mapping for its $h$ constituent Gaussians through a shared lighting-sensitive MLP $F_\theta$: \begin{equation}\{\tilde{\mathbf{c}}_j\}_{j=1}^h= F_\theta(\{\hat{\mathbf{c}}_j\}_{j=1}^h, \mathbf{\varepsilon}_a^{(i)}, \mathbf{\varepsilon}_l^{(v)}, \vec{d}_{iv}) , \label{eq:color-mapping}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{\varepsilon _l^{(v)}$ is the view-specific light embedding from input image $C_v$, following previous literature \cite{martin2021nerfw,kulhanek2024wildgaussians,dahmani2024swag .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-256: To maintain view consistency after color mapping, we also input the viewing direction $\vec{d _{iv}$ into $F_\theta$, aligned with the raw color prediction process (\cref{eq:neural_gaussian_color ).}}
elements.sent-2411-257: Through tile rasterization, both raw colors $\hat{\mathbf{c }$ and mapped colors $\tilde{\mathbf{c }$ are projected onto image planes for loss computation.}}
elements.sent-2411-258: By binding color transformations to kernel-level appearance embeddings rather than individual Gaussians, our method not only accelerates convergence through parameter sharing but also enhances representational capacity by facilitating appearance embeddings $\{\mathbf{\varepsilon _a^{(i)}\}$ to encode material-aware illumination responses and structural coherence.}
elements.sent-2411-259: This centralized paradigm contrasts sharply with conventional scattered embedding approaches, where isolated per-Gaussian parameters struggle to capture non-local lighting effects.
elements.sent-2411-260: Critically, the fixed-channel color expression provides a more stable optimization landscape compared to spherical harmonics with dynamic channels that are incompatible with neural network-based color mapping.
elements.sent-2411-261: This section elaborates on our structure-aware occlusion handling framework that bridges 2D uncertainty estimation with 3D representations to improve the accuracy of occlusion captures.
elements.sent-2411-262: In particular, our design leverages the 3D uncertainty estimation that grounds occlusion reasoning in geometric primitives and boundary-aware refinement that resolves semantic inconsistencies at scene peripheries.
elements.sent-2411-263: Central to this design is the prediction of 3D uncertainties $\{\mathbf{\beta _i\}$ for individual Gaussians through nexus kernels.}
elements.sent-2411-264: Learning an uncertainty embedding $\mathbf{\varepsilon _\beta^{(i)}$, each nexus kernel $\mathcal{U _i$ produces per-Gaussian confidence scores through a shared MLP $F_\beta$: \begin{equation}\{\mathbf{\beta}_j\}_{j=1}^h= F_\beta(\mathbf{\varepsilon}_\beta^{(i)}, \mathbf{\varepsilon}_\tau^{(v)}) , \label{eq:uncertainty predicting}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{\varepsilon _\tau$ is the transient embedding for recording image-specific occlusion patterns.}}}
elements.sent-2411-265: These 3D uncertainties $\{\mathbf{\beta _i\}$ are projected onto 2D image planes via tile rasterization: \begin{equation}\hat{\mathbf{\beta}}(\mathbf{x}')=\sum_{j\in h\times N}\beta_j\sigma_j\prod_{k=1}^{j-1}(1-\sigma_k), \label{eq:uncertainty propagation}\end{equation} where $\hat{\mathbf{\beta (\mathbf{x ')$ denotes the 2D uncertainty of pixel $\mathbf{x '$ and $\sigma_j$ follows the same blending weights as color rendering (\Cref{eq:splatting ).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-266: This uncertainty propagation mechanism effectively suppresses false positives in textureless regions by grounding occlusion reasoning in geometric confidences derived from the 3D representation, enabling robust occlusion segmentation in complex outdoor scenes with dynamic lighting and transient objects.
elements.sent-2411-267: Due to the lack of observations in training sets, the scene boundaries of the 3D representation often present lower rendering quality, leading to semantic discrepancies between rendered and ground-truth images and misclassifications by image models.
elements.sent-2411-268: To address the misclassifications at scene boundaries, we introduce a boundary-aware refinement modeled as an anisotropic 2D Gaussian distribution: \begin{equation}\mathcal{M}(\mathbf{x}') = e^{-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}'-\mathbf{\mu}')^\top\Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}'-\mathbf{\mu}')}, \label{eq:boundary refinement}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{\mu '$ and $\Sigma$ are estimated via DINO \cite{oquab2023dinov2 features, determining the rough scope of the foreground of input images.}}
elements.sent-2411-269: This refinement modulates uncertainty values near scene peripheries, reducing sensitivity in ambiguous regions while preserving occlusion accuracy in well-observed areas.
elements.sent-2411-270: Our training objective integrates a color loss $\mathcal{L _\mathrm{c}$ and an uncertainty loss $\mathcal{L _\mathrm{u}$ to jointly optimize geometry, appearance, and uncertainty reasoning.}}
elements.sent-2411-271: The color loss combines structural and perceptual similarity metrics through a weighted sum of DSSIM \cite{wang2004ssim and $L_1$ losses, dynamically modulated by predicted uncertainty mask $\frac{1 {2\hat{\mathbf{\beta ^2\mathcal{M }$ to attenuate contributions from occluded regions: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_c=\frac{\lambda}{2\hat{\mathbf{\beta}}^2\mathcal{M}}\mathrm{DSSIM}(\hat{\mathbf{C}},\mathbf{C}) + \frac{1-\lambda}{2\hat{\mathbf{\beta}}^2\mathcal{M}}L_1(\hat{\mathbf{C}},\mathbf{C}), \label{eq:color-loss}\end{equation} where $\lambda$ is set to 0.2 as a hyper-parameter.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-272: The uncertainty loss $\mathcal{L _\mathrm{u}$ enhances occlusion sensitivity through a hybrid formulation: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_u=\frac{\mathcal{D}(\hat{\mathbf{C}},\mathbf{C})}{2\hat{\mathbf{\beta}}^2} + \lambda_1\mathrm{log}\hat{\beta}, \label{eq:uncertainty-loss}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{D (\cdot)$ measures cosine similarity between DINO \cite{oquab2023dinov2 features of rendered and ground-truth images, and $\lambda_1=0.5$ controls the trade-off between uncertainty regulation and semantic consistency.}}}

elements.sent-2411-301: In this work, we introduced NexusSplats, an efficient framework for high-fidelity 3D scene reconstruction under complex lighting and occlusion conditions.
elements.sent-2411-302: By introducing hierarchical light decoupling, our method centralizes appearance learning through hierarchical Gaussian management, significantly reducing the number of parameters and accelerating training while preserving texture fidelity.
elements.sent-2411-303: The proposed structure-aware occlusion pipeline bridges 3D and 2D structural discrepancies via uncertainty propagation and boundary-aware refinement, eliminating persistent artifacts in prior works.
elements.sent-2411-304: Experimental results validate the superiority of NexusSplats in both efficiency and reconstruction quality.
elements.sent-2411-305: Our approach marks a critical step toward practical, high-fidelity 3D reconstruction for real-world applications.

elements.sent-2411-327: While NexusSplats achieves state-of-the-art performance in large-scale outdoor scenes with coupled lighting and occlusion challenges, its quantitative results on the NeRF On-the-Go dataset --- a benchmark focusing on small-scale indoor scenes with graded occlusion levels --- lag behind specialized baselines like WildGauss \cite{kulhanek2024wildgaussians .}
elements.sent-2411-328: This discrepancy stems from two factors inherent to our design priorities and evaluation metrics.
elements.sent-2411-329: First, traditional metrics like PSNR, SSIM \cite{wang2004ssim , and LPIPS \cite{zhang2018unreasonable measure global photometric fidelity rather than occlusion-specific accuracy.}}
elements.sent-2411-330: As shown qualitatively in \Cref{fig:quality2 , our method successfully removes transient occlusions (e.g. moving objects in "Patio-High") but incurs slight blurring in boundary regions.}
elements.sent-2411-331: NexusSplats prioritizes occlusion robustness over high-frequency detail preservation in small-scale indoor settings.
elements.sent-2411-332: Second, the hierarchical representation \cite{lu2024scaffold , tailored for efficient large-scale scene reconstruction via voxel-aligned kernels, struggles to resolve fine textures in confined indoor spaces.}
elements.sent-2411-333: This architectural bias toward outdoor scenarios leads to suboptimal parameter tuning for small-scale geometry.
elements.sent-2411-334: Nevertheless, our approach maintains competitive occlusion localization (e.g. 23.41 PSNR in "Corner" vs. WildGauss’s 23.46) while requiring fewer parameters, underscoring its suitability for real-world applications where efficiency and occlusion handling outweigh synthetic benchmark optimization.
elements.sent-2411-335: We visualize the impact of key components by progressively removing the hierarchical light decoupling module (w/o light.), uncertainty propagation (w/o uncert.), and boundary-aware refinement (w/o refine.).
elements.sent-2411-336: As shown in \Cref{fig:quality ablation1 , the full NexusSplats framework (rightmost column) restores photometric stability and geometric precision, closely matching ground truth.}
elements.sent-2411-337: These visual comparisons underscore the necessity of all three modules for robust in-the-wild reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-338: Removing the uncertainty propagation module (w/o uncert.) severely impacts occlusion handling across diverse indoor scenes.
elements.sent-2411-339: In the Corner and Spot sequences, transient objects leave residual artifacts in rendered views, as the model fails to distinguish static structures from occlusions.
elements.sent-2411-340: The Spot scene exhibits blurred boundaries around furniture legs due to unchecked uncertainty accumulation.
elements.sent-2411-341: Similarly, in outdoor-like setups (Mountain and Patio), missing uncertainty propagation learns occlusion geometries and erodes terrain details.
elements.sent-2411-342: The full NexusSplats framework restores precision while preserving intricate textures in partially observed areas.
elements.sent-2411-343: These results validate that our uncertainty propagation mechanism is indispensable for robustly aligning 3D geometry with 2D observations, particularly in scenes with dense transient elements or complex lighting interplay.
elements.sent-2411-344: The results showcase our method's ability to adapt reconstructed scene colors to match diverse target lighting conditions derived from the ground images.
elements.sent-2411-345: For each scene, six lighting conditions are visualized from three different viewpoints.
elements.sent-2411-346: The consistent adaptation across lighting conditions highlights the effectiveness of the light decoupling module in generalizing across scenes and conditions.
elements.sent-2411-347: While NexusSplats significantly improves occlusion handling through 3D uncertainty propagation and boundary-aware refinement, its current formulation exhibits limitations in scenarios requiring fine-grained scene completion.
elements.sent-2411-348: Excluding occluded pixels from the optimization process inherently halts color learning in masked regions, occasionally introducing visible gaps or artifacts in rendered views --- a trade-off inherent to occlusion-filtering frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-349: This issue is exacerbated in densely occluded urban scenes, where transient objects mask large contiguous areas, leaving under-optimized "holes" that degrade perceptual quality.
